sentences.text: I was born in Boskovice, Czechoslovakia, in 1918.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My family lived in Poland in a small town by the name of Zasow.
During the war, First World War, my family was evacuated from my hometown, Zasow, into Czechoslovakia, for safety reason.
Because the front line was there.
And it was dangerous to remain.
So the government, or whoever was in charge at the time, transferred the population from Zasow to Czechoslovakia.
My family wound out-- up in Boskovice, where I was born July 10, 1918.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1921, my family moved back to Zasow, where I remained throughout my youth.
My father, Jacob Perlmutter, was born in 1989.

And the first recollection that I have is that when I was almost three years old, or a little less, when there was still war going on between the Ukrainians and the Poles, I was hidden in a basement with my family.
And I wanted a drink of water.
So my father went up into-- upstairs to get me a drink of water.
And a shrapnel hit the house.
And for quite a while, my father did not appear back into the basement.
And we thought we-- he might have gotten hit.
But, thank God, he survived.
And that's how I remember.
My first recollection was the war between the Poles and the Ukrainians, the beginning of 1921.
Yes.
My sister was the oldest in the family.
She was born in 1914.
My brother, Max, was born in 1922.
And I, as I mentioned before, was born July 10, 1918.
My father was a very educated man.
He was-- professionally, he was a Hebrew teacher-- a graduate Hebrew teacher.
And he met my mother in the same school.
She was a trained and graduate nursery school teacher.
They made a living from owning a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], a, a home that was rented out to people to sleep a night over, or a week.
My mother used to bake and cook and do all kinds of chores.
She was a exceptional housewife and cook.
She had her own smoke house.
She used to make her own ice cream.
And this was the way they made a living.
With us in the house lived my grandmother, Leah, that passed away at a-- well, in her 80s-- in the beginning of the 80s in 1935.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Zasow was a unique little town.
There was many Jewish organizations.
At the time I lived there, there were about 1,500 Jews living in a town for about 3,000, total population.
I'm sorry?
Half or slightly over half was the Jew-- a Jewish population.

Now, the, the people lived-- made a living out of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] They used to make the silver ornaments for the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] It was a unique place-- one and only place in the world that was making those [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] The whole town was making a living from it until the Depression came.
Those [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] were sold mainly in the United States, where people could afford the expense.
The thread and the silver was brought from Germany.
Now, I'll give you a little background story about my family.
My great-grandfather was the one that came from Russia and settled in Zasow.
He made a factory of people that were doing this kind of artisan's work-- this kind of artistic work, which was called [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And they were making these-- weaving those [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
He had three grandsons-- three sons-in-law.
And for many years, this was the only factory in town.
But then the sons-in-laws could not get along very well.
So they separated.
And each one of them went into the same business, but on their own.
So the factory was dissolved as a unit.
But the people were still working and making a living from this art.
The second most important thing about Zasow is that we had a famous rabbi.
Everybody in, in the Jewish world knew about the Zasow rabbi.
Hasidic rabbi-- the, the all-time rabbi was [INAUDIBLE].
And he drew Hasidim from around the world, from all over the world.
During my period when I lived there, the rabbi's name was [INAUDIBLE].
And he was also very well-known around the world rabbi with a lot of influence.
Zasow was also unique because there were many Zionist organizations.
My father was one the leaders in the original-- organize of [?
Hovevei Zion. ?] That's in the beginning of the Zionist movement.
And my sister belonged to [INAUDIBLE].
And I leaned a little more towards the religious.
And I was a member of Bnei Akiva.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
My father passed away 25 days in the month of Adar in 1934.
He had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] pneumonia.
At that time, we had no penicillin.
He struggled for several days.
And he passed away at a very young age.
He was only 45, 46, right?
And how old were you at that time?
was 16.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my father-- You wanted to know, probably, about my schooling.
Well, I went through public school for eight years.
I was accepted to gymnasium.
But times in Poland at that time was very bad.
Jews were not permitted to go on to higher education.
It was called numerus nullus.
And my father was a ardent Zionist.
And he felt that I should not bother studying in Poland, but I should prepare myself to eventually go to Palestine.
He also wanted me to learn a trade, but he felt that I have a little bit of talent in drawings.
So he borrowed $50.
And I worked for a teacher in [?
Zasow ?] by the name of Ephraim Nachimowicz who got $50 for his [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] work.
And I worked for a year without pay.
I was an apprentice.
What else would you like to know?
OK.
I was orthodox, not--
Not Hasidic.
I was orthodox.
I mentioned to you that I-- my father wanted me to go to Palestine.
So shortly after his death, I went to Kibbutz [INAUDIBLE] in Kosow.
I spent over a year to get a preparatory, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an, an obligatory training for eventually getting a certificate to go to Palestine.
I did all kinds of work, from picking fruits in the summertime to making [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], rugs.
And whenever they needed me to-- for any kind of work-- that's was the idea-- I was trained to go to Palestine to live on a kibbutz.
And this was a prerequisite of getting a certificate to go to Palestine.
I was never chosen and never got a certificate, because there were other people that were ahead of me.
And the British government at the time gave very few certificates.
They-- the British had the mandate over Palestine at the time.
And--
They had their own numerus clausus.
Well, yes.
Well, only a few people were permitted to go to Palestine.
I was not the lucky one.
And after my father's death, I also had to support-- help support my family.
At the time, I was doing a specialty work, which was painting labels on pharmaceutical bottles and jars.
The law required in Poland in those years-- in 1937, 1938-- that every pharmacy has permanent labels on their, on their medical jars.
And, and, and I was the only one that was recommended to do this kind of work.
Otherwise, they had to send those jars to the factory in Czechoslovakia or in Austria, where they were originally purchased.
So I was going from one pharmacy to another, throughout Galicia-- former Galicia in, in the part of Poland.
And I was doing that for several years and helping out my family and earning a living.
Well, the best to my recollection is that students in Lemberg and Lwow were beaten up and cut up with razor blades when they went to school.
And that was one of the reasons my father felt that's not a good place to study in, that the best thing to do is to go get ready to go to Palestine where a Jew would be considered equal.
This was mostly by the index-- the index?
Yes, yes.
Poland, generally, is anti-Semitic country.
And from my experience, if it wouldn't have been for the Poles or the Ukrainians, a lot of Jews would have been saved during the Holocaust.
Yes, it was worse.
I mean, it wasn't very good during Pilsudski's times.
But then later on, it was worst.
It was open anti-Semitism.
No, I never received it.
And in 1939, the war broke out between the-- the, the Germans attacked Poland.
And there was a agreement between the Soviet Union and the German government.
And in 1939, the Russian-- the Soviets came to our part of Poland and occupied it.
Right.
Well, there was talk about, about a war broke-- breaking out.
But we didn't know more about it and--
Yes, we did, we did have a radio.
We knew that the war broke out.
And in several days, Poland was run over.
But as I mentioned--
No.
The-- we woke up in the morning, and the Russians were there.
And they stayed with us from 1939 to 1941.
Not, not in those years.
No.
Zasow is about 60 kilometers from Lwow.
The closest large town was [INAUDIBLE].
And the, the other town nearby was Brody.
That's right.
And they stayed with us till 1941.
As I mentioned before, we had a large house where guests could be accommodated.
We had some Russians, engineers, that worked on the roads with-- between the Soviet Union and, and Lwow, Lemberg.
And they lived in our house.
And they told us many things that are going on in the Soviet Union that was not very flattering to the Soviets.
They were telling us stories that things are not as good as we are-- we were told.
Not, not at that time.
But we spoke Polish.
And one of the engineers was a Jew.
And he spoke Yiddish.
So we knew what's going on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1941, Passover-- the second day of Passover-- the Soviets drafted me to the army.
I was-- during the years of 1939 to 1941, I worked in an officer's club in [INAUDIBLE] as an artist.
I was painting portraits of Stalin, [INAUDIBLE], the whole [INAUDIBLE], and was making many slogans on canvases.
So that was my occupation.
I was paid for it.
And when they took me to the army in 1941 and Passover, I continued working in my profession.
In uniform-- they never trained me to be a soldier.
But in 1941, when the war between the Germans and the Russians broke out--
Correct.
A few days later, I was sent to fight the Germans.
I never knew how to put a bullet into a, into a gun.
They gave me a [INAUDIBLE]-- a gun that was from 1914.
And, and they--
A world-- I don't know exactly what year that--
Antiquated.
And on the train going towards the front line, they showed me how to put a bullet into-- I wasn't much of a soldier at all.
[LAUGHS] I found myself in the big battle of Smolensk.
Smolensk.
That's--
That's correct.
It was one of the most important [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] scenes of war that-- of course, I have never been in a war, myself.
But when I was carrying two pails of water and the Germans were sitting on the tree sniping at us, both pails of water were shot.
And I was left alive.
I survived it.
Empty pails of water.
And a couple days later, the whole front broke out in a tremendous fight.
And 20,000 Russians were taken prisoner.
On the way to prison camp in Smolensk, as I was driven on a, on a bus-- on a open truck-- to the camp, I jumped out-- off the truck and started walking home.
It took 28 days for me to reach my hometown.
Many things happened on the way.
I was caught in one town.
And I was threatened to be shot.
Because the night before, a partisan group that was operating in that area killed a local peasant.
And they thought I'm one of the group.
And they were going to kill me.
The wife of the mayor of that village made it possible for me to escape from the house where I was kept overnight.
And I ran away and kept on walking.
It took 28 arduous days to get to my hometown.
I ate--
I ate what I could find, find in the fields, or just begged wherever I could.
And whatever I could do, that's how I survived.
I survived many more months of this kind of experience after I found myself in the woods.
That was later on, after the concentration camps.
The returning to your home in Zasow from in the neighborhood of Smolensk.
OK.
I found out-- about 20 kilometers before I came to my hometown, which was in Brody-- and I had friends from my-- from the kibbutz living there.
I found out from them that my family is still intact and alive.
And the next morning after I slept the night over in Brody, I came to Zasow.
And people going back from labor camp in Zasow ran to notify my mother that I came back from the Soviet army.
And that was a disaster.
Because I couldn't go home anymore.
Because I was afraid that if they find out that I came back from the enemy camp, they'll get rid of me.
So I was hiding for a few days in the local pharmacy.
And then I-- in the next night or so, I was, I was brought-- my mother came to see me.

And they made arrangements with the local [INAUDIBLE] to keep me hidden behind a, a big closet in a [INAUDIBLE] so they couldn't find me-- until my brother-in-law, my brother-in-law was able to get-- legitimize my staying, my return home, and got me some false papers.
And I started working first in Kalinka, where I used to shovel [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] excrements from cows and other animals.
Kalinka was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it?
A, a rich man's-- An estate.
An estate.
And the Germans supplied-- took it over and they supplied-- the Judenrat, supplied them with workers.
After a while, I was transferred to the German radio station in [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah.
No, not at that time.
No.
My, my mother and family was still at home in their own house.
Yes.
This would be, like, in the summer of '40, '41.
That's correct.
And then they established in [INAUDIBLE] a radio station on, on the top of a mountain.
In the woods.
And I was sent there by the Judenrat to carry water.
No ghetto, just a Judenrat.
And that was the, the people that the Germans turned to for manpower or whatever their needs were.
If they needed money or they needed something, they turned to the Judenrat.
And the Judenrat provided that.
Oh, Aktions, Aktions came while I came-- when I came home.
And they took people.
And they were just shooting them up wherever they wanted.
It was like a sport.
But the people did not-- were not congregated in a ghetto.
At that time yet.
There was a labor camp in Zasow.
